speaker: interviewer
text: After your work and after this man was taken and put into clay--

speaker: interviewee
text: He wasn't. All right. Go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: What? Tell me. Tell me.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. OK. Now, I left when they took him on the mount. He had to go on the mountain, and it was clay.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right.

speaker: interviewee
text: A clay mountain. When you walk on it when it's wet, clay is wet, you get stuck. So his foot came out and the shoe was stuck there. And he wanted to go back, get his shoe. So his kapo, who worked outside, who watched-- these workers started hitting him, and they killed him, because he got word from the kapo inside, get rid of them. Don't let him come home alive, or you will be dead. So he had to kill him. Now, he never made it to the top. He was finished. They dropped him down. In the evening, they put him-- and everybody walked. They put him in something like-- like a lorry that they-- [? we can't ?] [? stop. ?] They took him home to the camp dead and everything was accounted. Fine. I was relieved personally, no more beatings. In his place took another man, had to have a man to replace him inside. It was a guy who liked me, just the opposite of him. And in fact, he liked me so much that when we stood lines, he was sharing the food out. You know, when we came to food, and I wanted-- because he never looked at the faces. He just looked at the kettle. You held your pot. He never looked. But they came before me three people. I went, ahem. I started coughing, ahem, to get his attention. He looked up and he saw me approaching. He went down, beautiful-- [? all ?] [? right. ?] He gave water for the soup, maybe two, three pieces. He gave me a bunch of potatoes.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there you go. You could never figure out what is right, the right thing to do, what is the wrong thing to do. This guy took a liking to me and I was glad the other one was dead, and he liked me. And I had it a little better. It wasn't the Garden of Eden, but the-- I had it a little better, got a little better food. And then what happened in that camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: All right. It came a time within a month or so, you know, that was already I think March of '45. Could be the beginning of March of '45, and the Russian front, again, got closer. They're finished with Europe. They already invaded Germany. And they came to our camp, and they had-- we had to evacuate the camp. Again, they gave us a loaf of bread with-- yeah, with the-- with a can of meat. You know. Didn't know how long. And they took us to trains, freight trains, again, to go. So we went there. The bread and the can of meat, I finished up, because it-- it was dangerous to save the bread. People killed you for the bread. So-- so if I had nothing, I wasn't afraid that another prisoner-- because they created gangs there in camps, strong people that took on the weak, took away. If you tried to save a slice today, leave for tomorrow, you hid it, somehow you wound up dead, and you had no bread on your body.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they took us to the trains. And we were traveling I don't know where, how far, how many-- I don't remember. It wasn't many days.

speaker: interviewee
text: A certain point, yeah, on the way, the trains while we're traveling, I don't know, a few days, we had the American planes bombarding the train. And they knew when the plane, because either by radio or what, and they stopped, and they ran out, and they hid themselves under the train, the steel wheels, the guards, the engineer who drove the train. We didn't have. We were-- either we jumped out or laid down on the ground. Anyway, people got killed. These were trains, I want you to know, freight trains, but not closed. Open. What you carry sand, gravel, or whatever, that you put on a-- these are open freight trains. That's what we were traveling in, open freight trains, rain or shine, whatever happened, that's what we were. Anyway, they were bombarded. People were killed. They tried to hit mostly the locomotive. But it didn't-- sometimes you miss. And machine guns, wherever they were shooting, they had the result-- it looked to me when I looked up like golden eggs. I said golden eggs are flying. That's-- that's how they-- they looked. And they killed lots of people. Once even a bomb dropped while I was laying in-- in a field. A bomb dropped, because when I laid down, it was one piece of metal. And all of a sudden, and I got up, I see my-- I covered myself with a blanket I had. I covered myself, and the blanket I see is burning. I took it off and brushed myself, and I see next to me, let's say five yards away, a hole, a hole dug in like this room, a deep hole. A bomb fell there. It exploded, but on the meadow. So the shrapnel, whatever got away, fell, started burning on me. But nothing bad. But we were bombarded.

speaker: interviewer
text: So while we were traveling there a few days, we came to a point where it was a German guard standing for the train halt. So naturally, a leader from the guards, whoever was there, they're like, what's going on here? They spoke. Can't go further here. Can't travel.

speaker: interviewee
text: The German-- the English or the French or the Americans approaching. We don't know. You've got to walk now on foot. OK. So everyone unloaded train, said, [GERMAN], everybody, out. You know, we started walking on foot. On foot, that was what you hear now the death march. Whoever couldn't keep up walking, the people on the end, they were weak, they fell behind, was shot and kicked in a ditch. The road that we walked on had two drainage ditches on each side, one on each side of the road. And they just kicked the people in there. Go over there, they shot them and just kicked them in. And while we were walking, obviously there were other people, groups ahead of us, because we saw already dead people with the striped uniforms laying there, so that means people walked before us here. It's important to note at one time while we were walking, we merged with another road, like a fork. We came. We merged with another group. They were also guarded by people, but all civilian clothing, shoes, real shoes, no-- no wood shoes. Civilian clothing, hats, every-- no marks from prisoners. And they started-- the guards from each group started talking amongst themselves. And they decided we should walk together, mix this group with ours, make one group from it. That-- that was their decision. So now we had civilians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were they Jewish? Were they Poles? You don't know.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no idea who they were, what they were, whether they committed crimes, because that's very unusual to see people walking. They looked like Germans, because their clothes were civilian clothes, but the hats, the winter hats-- I mean, everything's like Germans, but they must not have been Germans. I'll tell you later how we found that out. Anyway, we were walking for hours. Wherever we came, halt. German guards, halt. Can't go here. Here the French are coming. All right. Go back this way. We went a few kilometers the other way. Halt. Can't go. This one, Russians are coming. From every side, like we were already in the middle surrounded by foreign armies that we couldn't go. They decided to take us into the woods. There was a road, ditches, and trees. Woods, regular woods. And they took us in there. We had to sit down in a circle and sit there. I don't know. This could have been the second or the third day, because one night-- yeah. It must have been the second or the third, because while we were walking, when it came dark, they told us, lay down. We're going to stay here for the night.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it was in a meadow where it was a valley. I got up in the morning. Everything was wet. The ground was wet from the dew or from whatever. I was soaking wet. However, I was laying, that's [INAUDIBLE]. So I remember, it was more than one night that we were. When they took us into the woods, we were sitting there. At the-- About how many people?

speaker: interviewee
text: In the hundreds, but I don't know whether 300, 400. And the civilians among us, the same thing. From our camp, the people was merged these civilians. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Came in a few soldiers that was SS, because our guards were more like army people. Now came in SS with the deadheads they had marked in the SS, sleeves rolled up. We saw bloody arms, [INAUDIBLE].

speaker: interviewer
text: And they said to the guards, these are the [GERMAN], the [GERMAN]? Are these the actors, the musicians? They gave us a name. All of a sudden we came actors-- became actors and musicians. And that's all.

speaker: interviewee
text: They told our regular guards, step back. Now they were in the front. Our guards were in the back. They were in front of us with machine guns in their hands. Now they're saying to us, these guys, [SPEAKING GERMAN], meaning native Germans who were born in Germany. Germans who were born outside of Germany, step out. So a few guys stepped out, but not from the civilians. They told me they were not Germans, but yet they wear civilian dressed.

speaker: interviewer
text: And as soon as these people stepped out right behind them, they opened up with the machine guns on us. OK? Now, I happened to be in the front. [INAUDIBLE]. I had no chance to escape. People who were sitting farther out in the group, in the outer circle farther, started running in the woods. They started running.

speaker: interviewee
text: Me, I wasn't hurt. I fell down as dead. And they went through with the machine guns. There was no doubt in their minds that here, the people are dead. I fell down as dead. Later, after they left, I knew a few people fell down like I was, because we were associated. There was one fellow that I walked with him. But coming to that, after they shot us, they just left. We heard it's quiet, nobody talking. We knew they were left. But we also-- I-- we, I say. I realized somebody will have to come to clean this mess up, to bury us or whatever, and I got up. And I look and a fellow next to me, well, somebody stands up, he got up too. His name is Alter Rosenzweig. He lives in Brooklyn. I met him once, but I know he told me it was-- matter of fact, I got his phone number. He was a few years older from me. He must have been already in the military or maybe had some military preparations, because he knew a lot more than I did regarding things to survive. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] He said to me, let's get out of here. But first, we must change clothes. Let's get clothes from the civilians, grab, take off your uniform, because I was assigned in Dora already with the-- the [GERMAN], which means the striped uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, let's get out of this and get away from here, because they're gonna come sooner or later and bury these people. So if we stay, they're gonna bury us alive. While we were talking, another guy got up, which in fact lives now in Palm Beach here, Martin [? Wiener, ?] [INAUDIBLE] [? Wiener. ?] And he started turning the dead people over, my brother, my [? Vovek ?], where are you? [? Vovek ?] was his brother's name. He was there, but he escaped. So anyway, to make the story short, we changed. We took off our stuff. I put on civilians', good shoes. And we walked away in the woods, away from this mess.

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know. I wasn't walking too long, maybe 10 minutes, which couldn't have been a big distance away from this. And I noticed a German with a gun. Germans were planted in the woods. The big trees, they were standing behind trees. You couldn't-- oh, sorry. You couldn't possibly see them. And we walked, and I noticed a German, and I lay down as dead. But this guy came over to me with the-- with the rifle, poked, poked. [GERMAN], get up. I knew I was as good as dead.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he has been there for hours, obviously, because he asked me, who did the shooting there? So he knew, he heard machine guns in-- it was at this area. And I see a soldier. And I didn't want to say SS. I was afraid. I didn't know who he is. I said, soldiers did it. Now, a German in uniform, soldiers? No. Soldiers would never do that. [GERMAN] SS means the swines, the SS did that. I heard this. I was surprised, a German telling me he didn't know that I was from a concentration camp. He saw civilians walking, but he saw that we were off in there. What would we do in the woods? And our German, he could detect-- I learned pretty good German later. But he could detect we are foreigners. We are what? And he says, the German SS.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyway, even before that, I went, dropped on the floor, grabbed his feet, and started to kiss his shoes. I wanted to kiss, because I say the man is talking to me. He is not threatening me he's going to kill me. But as a soldier, he jumped back. You know. He said, no, no, no. This is not necessary, necessary. He said, the Americans are already in Bismarck. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Me, German, I don't know where I am. He tells me. I said, where is Bismarck, in German. He said, you got to walk straight in this direction. Don't get out of the woods. Stay in this direction straight you'll get to Bismarck. The Americans are there. Before-- before we left him, he told us, you see, this is a wooden leg this. I am not a foot soldier. I was in the Air Force. I was shot down over the English Channels, and since I can't fly no more, they put me in uniform to be as a guard. That was a German that didn't kill us. I want to emphasize that. 99 out of 100 were bad. You found one that let you live. Thanks to him, we survived so far.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we knew already the Americans are already in-- in near distance that they're here. The liberation could be soon. But we walked. And somehow the woods got narrower, so we could barely seeing from the road, we could be seen.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we noticed-- it was already maybe 3:00, 4:00 in the afternoon. I don't know. And we noticed a fellow with a P. And we knew this is a civilian worker that they took forced labor, civilian at the farm, a P. And P is Polish, because for everyone, they had a designated name, a letter.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we had no choice, so maybe-- we walked out of the woods, because civilian clothes, walked over to the guy. And the fellow that was with me he goes, right away, [NON-ENGLISH SPEECH], he crosses-- he knew more than I did. I was from a religious family. I didn't know much about this stuff.

speaker: interviewee
text: He walks over to this fellow and he tells him, hey, back there, a few kilometers, they were machine gunning us. We survived. We walked to here.

speaker: interviewer
text: He says, yes, the Americans are not far from here. You know what? You can go into the barn, but not now. When it gets-- stay there where you are till it gets dark.  I'll leave the gate unlocked. I wouldn't lock it.

speaker: interviewee
text: When it gets dark, the farmer wouldn't see you. He's only here with his wife, no children. Get in there quietly in the barn and hide yourself. We got straw, he says, very high, double or triple the high here, a mountain of straw. Get up there and hide. Now, we waited till it was dark. That fellow Alter and I, we went into that place and to open first the gate, then went into the barn. And we hide-- went up on the mountain, dug out a few bundles of straw, laid down, and put it on top of us, and we were underneath, couldn't be seen. OK. Now, we are hungry. But you lose your hunger when your danger-- your life is in danger, you don't think about it. That was Friday, still Friday, the 13th, April the 13th, they took us and shot us up. We were laying there overnight. We slept there. But we heard Friday evening coming in there in the barn, not on top of the straw but on the ground, we heard talking Germans. And we heard that they were soldiers, because they said, we've got to change uniforms into civilian clothes. Ooh. They got to change, then it's really close. But we were quiet. Obviously the farmer got them together some clothes, and they changed because early in the morning they left. And we were still there. They left Saturday morning. We were still there Saturday. Yes. Saturday all day, and we slept there Saturday night. However, in the evening, I went down, undug myself and went down the ground and nobody was there, heard this quiet, nobody there. And there was a-- a-- a wagon that a horse could pull, you know, full of potatoes, raw potatoes. I filled my pockets up with potatoes-- sorry-- went back up to my friend there, Alter.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, I got raw potatoes. Can you eat? He said, you can eat anything, as long as it's edible. I tell you, he must have had some-- some military training, because he knew.

speaker: interviewee
text: I bit into a potato. That was bad taste-- how do you say floury, juice. But I ate it anyway. We fell asleep. It was Sunday morning. But we were hungry, of course didn't taste so good. Monday morning-- Sunday morning-- now, it could have been-- it's either-- I can't say exactly, but it was Sunday already or Monday. I don't know how many nights we spent there. All I know, I was so hungry I said to him, look. Let's get out of here. I don't care. Let them shoot us. I can't take it anymore. Finish. Forget about it. We walked out from this barn early. We walked on the road. And on the road we found cigarettes, part-- partly smoked cigarettes. I picked it up. Germans usually smoke cigarettes, they were short tobacco, that they burned their fingernails. And this was already a little hint. I picked up, and I saw the brand is not a German brand. I said, Alter, they got here different armies. This area must be liberated or soldiers were here, because-- he says, yeah. This is no German cigarette, neither is it-- you know, he could tell a German wouldn't throw away half a cigarette. And American could get two puffs, go.

speaker: interviewer
text: We went, walked on that road straight. We saw from a distance a German tank. We recognized it by the cross they had on it. But we thought, should we go forward or should we back up?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, hey, we see them. They seen us. If we back up, they come to us. We've got to go now. We've got to be brave. I said, walk. We came to the tank. The chain was off one side. It was an abandoned tank. Lucky for us, nobody was there. And we walked a little further by a farm. And we have seen a-- a mulatto, an American. Now, I know, then they told us, American, American. You know, we didn't understand English, couldn't talk. We seen an American soldier, an officer. It was a mulatto. And we explained that we are from prison. Maybe he understood a little German. Maybe he did, because he said he'll send us to a camp. But we told him, this was-- they made the headquarters there on a German farm, in the house, in the farmhouse. They made the headquarters. He said, I'll send you. Yeah, but we are hungry now. He said, he'll go into German. And we left very early, so just we caught them for breakfast there. He sent us-- went into the farmer. He said he's got two guys here who are very hungry. Feed them breakfast. They prepared. He said, go in there. You know, took us. Language we didn't understand. I know now because he took us in. The farmer sit down and they put servings on the table. I didn't see that in years, you know, silverware, the stuff. They served scrambled eggs with bacon. [LAUGHS] Just the smell, I got full. And we started eating that. And then for dessert, she puts coffee and takes a cake. You know, they-- Germans, I don't know, bake thin cake. I don't know, not the high cake, very thin cake in a flat pan. And it was a pan twice as big as this here, twice.

speaker: interviewer
text: And she went to cut out a piece. We said, uh-uh, just leave it. [SPEAKING GERMAN] You know what we did? We took the knife, we split the cake in half. Alter took a half. We finished it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now the stomach is full. We go out. Mmm, yeah. You show them, it's good. We are happy. We've got a full stomach.

speaker: interviewer
text: So he told there was-- also Poles work, Poles or other foreigners worked at every farm. And he said-- and he said-- he said to the Pole, take them to the camp where the foreigners are. All right. We'll go to tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
